focus: Resolve seam continuity with minimal edits.
next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 4

Where, precisely, did the bombs begin? The question worked under everything else as Elise stood in the print chamber deeper than the sleeping gallery, where the chalk walls held the smell of ink and lamp oil the way old wool held rain. In Vautrin's office the answer had seemed indecently simple. A quarry. Printers. Wanted men. Put those nouns in the right order and the Prefecture could call the rest conspiracy before anyone had lit a fuse. She wanted the proof now. She wanted something harder than the memory of Henri Vasseur taken at the curb while she kept walking under another name.

Nadir stood at the nearest case slightly side-on to the room, one shoulder angled toward the passage, keeping the blind side under watch. Ink had gone so far into the creases of his hands it looked permanent. He had tucked a strip of spoiled proof into his cuff and, between one line and the next, was folding it with absent precision into a paper bird for Lucie, who waited in the passage with one stocking at her ankle and chalk dust caught in her lashes. Opposite him Abel worked the composing stick fast enough to make the metal click. He was narrow and unfinished, his cuffs too short, two fingertips glazed with old printer's burns. A violet carpenter's pencil rode behind one ear. Every few moments he pushed it back, leaving a faint smudge at his temple and buying himself a beat to think.

Clemence took the basket from Elise and dropped it on the table. Lye had roughened her knuckles into a red map, and one button on her skirt had been replaced with a loop of string. "If you're going to be useful, be useful with both hands," she said. "Nadir, give her the pied mess. Abel, stop courting the cases."

Nadir tipped a tray toward Elise. It held a ruin of letters, bent rules, wanderi...
prev_tail_excerpt:
..., female, approximately ten, present in the schoolroom and sleeping gallery.

The last line sat on the page with the lead still shining. From two alcoves over came Lucie's cough, dry and quick under the blanket, followed by Clemence's sleep-rough murmur telling her to turn on her side if she meant to keep that racket up. Elise put the pencil down. Her fingers had gone white around it anyway.

She read the three lines once, as Vautrin would read them: entrance, leader, proof that families lived below and could be counted, sorted, used. A schoolroom would become indoctrination in a charge sheet. The clinic would become sheltering fugitives. The sleeping alcoves would become a nest. Lucie would become a tally mark no one bothered to name before the boots came through.

Elise pressed the heel of her thumb over "One child, female, approximately ten" until the paper creased and the graphite smeared into a dark bruise. Then she drew a second line through the sentence so hard the tip snapped. The broken grit jumped into her lap. She folded the proof around the two surviving lines, tucked it under her thigh, and sat very still, listening to the quarry breathe around her.

She had come below to map a cell and name its master. By the end of one day she had mapped a household: a laundress who tied ribbon to a bucket so her daughter could keep order in a world built on shortage, a printer who folded birds out of spoiled proofs, a vain young compositor who still broke his bread smaller for a hungry child, a schoolmaster teaching fractions on stone because there was no slate and no sky, and a sick seamstress whose dressings were changed before anyone fed themselves.

Vautrin wanted conspirators. What he meant to crush was a whole social world that had learned to fit itself under rock.
